title: Daily Briefing — Sunday, June 21
tldr: Web-design play of the day: do scroll-reveal animations in pure CSS — fire an animation once an element enters the viewport, no IntersectionObserver, no JavaScript. Chrome shipped scroll-triggered animations (Chrome 145/146, 2026) — the missing half of scroll animation. Scroll-driven animation scrubs an animation to your scroll position; scroll-triggered plays a normal, timed animation once when the element crosses a threshold (think CSS's own Intersection Observer). Two new properties do it: timeline-trigger: --t view() entry 100% exit 0% defines when it fires, and animation-trigger: --t play-forwards ties your @keyframes to that trigger. Action keywords cover the lot — play-forwards, play-backwards, play-once, pause, reset, replay. It's Chromium-only today (Firefox/Safari not yet), so keep your IntersectionObserver as the fallback and treat it as progressive enhancement — but it deletes a whole category of reveal-on-scroll JS for most of your traffic.

Graphic play: re-direct footage you already shot — change the camera angle, swap the environment, or restyle a clip from a reference image — inside Figma, no reshoot. On Jun 18 2026 Figma put Runway's Aleph 2.0 model into Figma Weave: frame-level video direction on the design canvas. It edits clips up to 30 seconds (a full scene, not a 2-second sample), takes a reference image to apply a look while preserving everything you didn't ask to change, and carries keyframe edits through every frame a subject appears in. The operator move: stop re-filming for every variant — drop a clip on the Weave canvas, type the change, preview before you commit. (Pricing is shifting to scale with input length; check current cost before batch-running client work.)

Growth play: stop spending time on llms.txt for AI visibility — the data says almost nobody reads it — and start tracking your real AI-citation share where you actually can. Ahrefs analyzed 137,000 domains (study Jun 15 2026): 97% of llms.txt files got zero requests in a month, and AI bots were ~1% of all fetches; Google's John Mueller says a self-reported file 'can't help an LLM tell one site from another.' Meanwhile Bing Webmaster Tools shipped AI Citation Share (preview, global, Jun 19 2026) — the percentage of AI citations your site captures for a grounding query, plus how you stack up against competitors (Bing/Copilot only; Google Search Console still has no citation metric). Reallocate the llms.txt hour into clean HTML structure, internal links, and one-sentence answers up top — then watch your Citation Share move.

CRO/AOV play (answering the team's open question, sharper this time): set your free-shipping threshold by contribution margin, not a round number above AOV — and give mobile its own, lower threshold. The folklore rule ('AOV + 15–25%') can quietly lose money. The honest math: (Threshold − AOV) × gross-margin% = the extra margin a nudged order earns; your threshold makes sense only when that covers the shipping you're giving away. And because mobile AOV runs ~20–30% lower than desktop, one blended threshold leaves phone shoppers staring at a goal they can't reach — so set a separate, lower mobile bar. Keep the live 'you're $X away' progress bar (still the highest-ROI single AOV widget); just stop picking the number by vibes. Context: Deloitte's 2026 outlook found 67% of retail execs plan to raise their threshold this year — re-peg yours to current AOV and re-run the margin math.

Sharpen your edge: today's throughline is do less of the busywork the platform doesn't reward, and put the saved effort where the signal is. Don't hand-write reveal-on-scroll JS the browser now does in two CSS lines; don't re-shoot a clip you can re-direct on the canvas; don't tend an llms.txt file 97% of bots ignore; don't pick a shipping threshold by gut when a one-line margin formula tells you the real number. Each is the same move — delete the ritual, keep the result, and aim the freed-up hour at the lever that actually pays.

sections.h: Web design — run it today: fire reveal-on-scroll animations from pure CSS (no IntersectionObserver) with scroll-triggered animations

blocks.sub: Chrome shipped scroll-TRIGGERED animations (Chrome 145/146, 2026) — the counterpart to scroll-driven animation: a normal timed animation that plays ONCE when an element crosses a viewport threshold, declared with two new properties (timeline-trigger + animation-trigger) and zero JavaScript
blocks.p: What changed: Chrome shipped scroll-triggered animations (announced for Chrome 145, shipping in the 145/146 stable wave, 2026). This is the half of scroll animation that was missing. Scroll-driven animation (already Baseline-ish in Chromium) scrubs an animation's progress to how far you've scrolled — great for progress bars and parallax. Scroll-triggered animation is different: it plays an ordinary, time-based @keyframes animation once, the moment an element crosses a scroll threshold — exactly the 'reveal this card as it scrolls into view' pattern teams currently hand-build with JavaScript's IntersectionObserver. Two new CSS properties do the work. timeline-trigger (longhands timeline-trigger-name / timeline-trigger-source) names a trigger and points it at a timeline — e.g. timeline-trigger: --t view() entry 100% exit 0% means 'fire when the element has fully entered the view and reset when it has fully exited.' Then animation-trigger: --t play-forwards binds your animation to that trigger. The action keywords cover the full range: play-forwards, play-backwards, play-once, pause, reset, replay, none. Why it matters for us: reveal-on-scroll is on nearly every marketing site we ship, and today it costs a JS bundle, event wiring, and a class of bugs (observers that never disconnect, animations that re-fire on every scroll jiggle). Moving it into the stylesheet makes it declarative, designer-editable, and cheaper — and because an unsupported browser simply shows the element in its final state, it degrades cleanly.
blocks.doIt: (1) Write the keyframes + animation as normal: @keyframes fade-up { from { opacity:0; translate:0 1rem } to { opacity:1; translate:0 0 } } and on the element animation: fade-up .35s ease both;. (2) Define the trigger: add timeline-trigger: --reveal view() entry 100% exit 0%; (the view() timeline + entry/exit range decides exactly when it fires). (3) Bind the animation to it: add animation-trigger: --reveal play-forwards; — now the animation runs once when the element enters, instead of immediately on load. (4) Pick the behaviour: use play-once for a one-time reveal, or play-forwards/play-backwards for enter/leave pairs. (5) Keep a fallback for non-Chromium: wrap the JS path in @supports not (animation-trigger: --x play-forwards) (or feature-detect) and keep your existing IntersectionObserver reveal for Firefox/Safari — or just let elements render in their final state there (no animation, no breakage). (6) Try it on one section (a testimonials row or feature grid) before ripping out your animation library. Card: card-webdesign-scroll-triggered-animations.
blocks.note: Grounding: the property names (timeline-trigger/animation-trigger), the action keywords, and the 'plays once vs scrubs' distinction are from Chrome for Developers' own announcement and an independent CSS-Tricks first-look (Geoff Graham); utilitybend covers the same feature independently. Honest scope: this is Chromium-only right now — Chrome for Developers' demo explicitly ships an IntersectionObserver fallback, and the spec is still settling (treat exact range syntax as subject to change), so ship it as progressive enhancement, not a dependency you delete for all users (that's the Baseline test we keep applying). Joins thread-modern-css-primitives (the Chromium-only branch: carousel, sibling-index, gap-decorations). Logged a prediction (a 2nd engine ships animation-trigger → Baseline path). Sources: Chrome for Developers, CSS-Tricks, utilitybend.

sections.h: Graphic — run it today: re-direct video you already shot (camera angle, environment, restyle from a reference) inside Figma with Runway Aleph 2.0 in Weave — no reshoot

blocks.sub: Figma put Runway's Aleph 2.0 model into Figma Weave (Jun 18 2026): frame-level video editing on the design canvas — edit clips up to 30s, apply a look from a reference image while preserving everything else, and carry a keyframe edit through every frame a subject appears in
blocks.p: What changed: on Jun 18 2026 Figma integrated Runway's Aleph 2.0 video model into Figma Weave, its generative canvas — bringing 'direct every frame' editing to where brand and design work already happens. Aleph 2.0 is built for control rather than one-shot generation: it edits clips up to 30 seconds (long enough to direct a full scene, not just a 2-second sample); you can bring in a reference image to inform the look and it applies that across the video while preserving everything you didn't ask to change; and keyframe edits carry through — change a subject in one frame and the edit follows that subject wherever it appears. Once a clip is on the canvas you can change the camera angle, introduce a new character, or transform the environment entirely — without a reshoot, previewing each change before you commit in a connected, iterative workflow. Why it matters for us: video is now table-stakes for brand and social, and the expensive part has always been re-shooting for every variant, aspect ratio, or client note. This collapses 'we need another angle / a different background / an on-brand restyle' from a production day into a canvas edit, in the same Figma file where the rest of the creative lives — so the designer, not just the editor, can iterate. (Frames the durable shift: AI video is moving from 'generate a clip' to 'direct the clip you have.')
blocks.doIt: (1) Open Figma Weave and drop your existing clip (or a generated one) onto the canvas. (2) Restyle from a reference: add a reference image and prompt the look you want — Aleph 2.0 applies it while keeping untouched elements intact (good for matching a campaign's grade/palette). (3) Re-direct the scene: prompt a new camera angle, a swapped environment, or an added element instead of scheduling a reshoot. (4) Use keyframe edits when a change needs to track a subject across the shot — set it once and let it carry through the frames. (5) Preview, then commit: review each change in the connected workflow before finalizing; iterate on the parts that miss. (6) Check cost first: Figma says pricing is moving to scale with input length — confirm current credits/pricing before batch-running variants for a client. Card: card-graphic-runway-aleph-figma-weave.
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Figma's own blog ('Direct Every Frame with Runway Aleph 2.0, Now in Figma Weave', primary) + Runway's Aleph 2.0 product page (independent confirmation of the model's capabilities). Honest scope: pricing is explicitly in flux ('will be updated soon to scale with input length') — treat cost as a check-before-you-run item; outputs still need a human eye for artifacts and for likeness/rights when you add or alter people. Confirm commercial-use terms for client deliverables. Advances the graphic AI-creative thread from 'train the brand + generate' toward 'direct existing footage'; related to card-graphic-firefly-brand-batch-creatives (the brand-trained-generation play). Logged a prediction (a 2nd major design/creative tool ships in-canvas frame-level AI video direction). Sources: Figma Blog, Runway.

sections.h: Growth — run it today: drop llms.txt as an AI-visibility tactic (97% of files go unread) and track your real AI-citation share in Bing Webmaster Tools instead

blocks.sub: New data (Ahrefs, 137,000 domains, Jun 15 2026): 97% of llms.txt files got zero requests in a month and AI bots were ~1% of fetches — Google's Mueller says a self-reported file can't differentiate sites; meanwhile Bing Webmaster Tools shipped AI Citation Share (preview, global, Jun 19 2026), reporting the % of AI citations your site wins for a query vs competitors
blocks.p: What changed: two things landed this week that re-point AEO (Answer Engine Optimization — getting named in AI answers) effort. (1) The llms.txt myth got measured. Ahrefs analyzed 137,000 domains (study published Jun 15 2026) and found 97% of llms.txt files received zero requests in the month studied; AI retrieval bots were only ~1% of total fetches, and of the few requests that did hit those files, the large majority were generic bots, not citation engines. Google's John Mueller added the why: because llms.txt is self-reported, it 'can't help an LLM tell one site from another,' and Google's generative-AI guidance says machine-readable files like it aren't needed to appear in AI search. Translation: publishing llms.txt is cheap and harmless, but it is not a lever that moves AI visibility right now — so stop budgeting time for it. (2) AI-citation measurement got more first-party. Bing Webmaster Tools rolled out an AI Citation Share report (preview, global, Jun 19 2026) inside its AI Performance dashboard: it shows the percentage of AI citations your site captures for a given grounding query, plus a competitive comparison, alongside new Intents and Topics groupings. The catch: it covers Bing/Copilot answers only — Google Search Console still surfaces no AI-citation metric. Why it matters for us: this sharpens the AEO loop we've been teaching. The work that pays is clean HTML structure, internal links, and genuinely good one-sentence answers up top — not protocol files crawlers ignore. Spend the reclaimed hour there, and use Citation Share (where your audience uses Bing/Copilot) plus the visibility checkers we already use to confirm it landed.
blocks.doIt: (1) Stop investing in llms.txt for visibility: leave any existing file in place (it's harmless), but remove it from your AEO to-do list and don't sell it to clients as a ranking/citation lever. (2) Turn on AI Citation Share: in Bing Webmaster Tools → AI Performance, open the Citation Share report and read your share of AI citations for your key grounding queries, plus the competitor comparison. (3) Pair it with cross-engine checks: Bing-only data isn't the whole picture — keep running a free AI-visibility check (card-growth-ai-visibility-check) for ChatGPT/Gemini/Perplexity and read a few real AI answers by hand. (4) Redirect the effort to what's measured to work: clean heading/HTML structure, strong internal links, an intent-matched title, a one-sentence answer at the top, and FAQ/Article schema (card-growth-schema-aeo, card-growth-aeo-content-formats). (5) Re-measure Citation Share in ~2–4 weeks to confirm your structural pass moved your share. Card: card-growth-ai-visibility-check (updated).
blocks.note: Corroboration: Reported (2) — Search Engine Journal's SEO Pulse (covers both the Bing Citation Share launch and the llms.txt data) + Ahrefs' own 137k-domain study (primary for the 97%/1% figures). Honest scope: Citation Share is Bing/Copilot-only and in preview — it is not a read on Google's AI Overviews/AI Mode, where there's still no first-party citation metric, so don't treat it as total AI visibility. The llms.txt finding is 'not useful for citation today,' not 'will never matter' — the standard (Microsoft) is young; some still publish it defensively. Advances thread-answer-engine-optimization (optimize → measure → mark-up, now: don't over-invest in ignored protocol files). Logged a prediction (Google Search Console ships an AI-citation/answer-visibility metric matching Bing's Citation Share). Sources: Search Engine Journal, Ahrefs.

sections.h: CRO & AOV — run it today: set your free-shipping threshold by contribution margin (not a round number above AOV), and give mobile its own lower bar

blocks.sub: The 'AOV + 15–25%' folklore can quietly lose money; the honest test is (Threshold − AOV) × gross-margin% ≥ the shipping you give away — and because mobile AOV runs ~20–30% lower than desktop, a single blended threshold leaves phone shoppers chasing a goal they rarely reach, so set a separate mobile threshold (re-answers the team's standing CRO/AOV question, sharper)
blocks.p: What changed / why resurface it: the team's open Ask-anything question — 'the best one thing to focus on for CRO and AOV' — is back in the demand backlog, so here's the sharper answer than the first pass. The single highest-ROI AOV widget is still the free-shipping threshold + a live 'you're $X away' progress bar (the goal-gradient effect: people push harder as a reward gets closer). What changes today is how you pick the number. The popular rule — set the bar ~15–25% above your current AOV — is a fine starting hypothesis but it ignores margin, and a threshold that lifts AOV while you eat shipping can be a net loss. The honest method is contribution-margin math: (Threshold − AOV) × gross-margin% = the incremental margin a nudged order earns, and the threshold only makes sense when that incremental margin at least covers the per-order shipping you're now giving away. Second correction: mobile AOV runs roughly 20–30% lower than desktop, so one blended threshold sets a goal most phone shoppers can't reach — quietly wasting the nudge on your largest traffic source. Set a separate, lower mobile threshold. Why it matters for us: this is the difference between an AOV tactic that reads well in a deck and one that survives a P&L. The mechanism (goal-gradient) is settled; the number is a margin calculation and a device segmentation, not a vibe. (Context: Deloitte's 2026 retail outlook found 67% of execs plan to raise their threshold this year against carrier/tariff cost shocks — a stale number is likely now too low and wrong on margin.)
blocks.doIt: (1) Pull the inputs: last-90-day AOV, gross-margin %, and your real average shipping cost per order — split by device (mobile vs desktop). (2) Run the margin test, not the round number: for a candidate threshold, compute (Threshold − AOV) × gross-margin% and only keep thresholds where that covers the shipping you'd absorb on a nudged order. (3) Set a separate mobile threshold ~20–30% below your desktop number so phone shoppers see a goal they can actually hit. (4) Keep the progress bar in cart/mini-cart ('You're $12 away from free shipping') with one relevant low-cost add-on beside it — that widget is the lift; the threshold is just the target. (5) AI assist: export your order-value distribution and ask Claude/ChatGPT for the threshold that captures the most 'one-nudge-away' orders at or above your margin floor, per device, and to draft the bar copy. (6) Measure all three for 3–4 weeks — AOV, conversion, AND shipping-cost margin — before locking it; re-peg annually (the 2026 reset). Card: card-cro-free-shipping-threshold (updated).
blocks.note: Corroboration: the margin-math method + the mobile-vs-desktop AOV gap come from Digital Applied's 2026 free-shipping playbook (contribution-margin framing; cites Deloitte 67% and Baymard ~48% surprise-cost abandonment) and Growth Suite's AOV guide (mobile AOV ~20–30% lower); Baymard, Capital One Shopping, and Deloitte back the underlying CRO/AOV stats. Honest flag: the specific percentages (15–25% above AOV; 20–30% mobile gap; ~15–20% progress-bar lift) are well-supported starting points to TEST on your store, not guarantees — the durable rule is 'watch the margin, not just AOV/CVR.' Advances the cro 'two highest-leverage levers' thread (refines the threshold method rather than repeating it); related: card-cro-show-total-cost-early, card-cro-post-purchase-upsell, card-cro-cross-sell-bundles. Sources: Digital Applied, Growth Suite, Baymard, Capital One Shopping, Deloitte.

sources: Chrome for Developers — CSS scroll-triggered animations are coming! · CSS-Tricks — A First Look at Scroll-Triggered Animations · utilitybend — CSS Animation Triggers: playing animations on scroll without scrubbing · Figma Blog — Direct Every Frame with Runway Aleph 2.0, Now in Figma Weave · Runway — Aleph 2.0 · Search Engine Journal — SEO Pulse: AI Citation Share ships, new data doubts llms.txt · Ahrefs — We analyzed 137K sites: 97% of llms.txt files never get read · Search Engine Journal — 97% of llms.txt files got no requests, Ahrefs data shows · Digital Applied — Free Shipping Threshold Strategy 2026 · Growth Suite — Free Shipping Threshold Strategy to Increase AOV · Baymard Institute — Cart Abandonment Rate · Deloitte — 2026 Retail Industry Outlook
